wikipedia_title: Type I and type II errors
display_title: Type I and type II errors

annotations.label: Type I error (false positive)

anchor.snippet: is the incorrect rejection of a true null hypothesis

annotations.note: Mathlib has measure-theoretic probability but no formalization of statistical hypothesis testing or type I errors.

annotations.label: Type II error (false negative)

anchor.snippet: is the incorrect acceptance of a false null hypothesis

annotations.note: No hypothesis-testing framework exists in Mathlib, so type II errors are not formalized.

annotations.label: Null and alternative hypothesis

anchor.snippet: two competing propositions called null hypothesis

annotations.note: Grep found no notion of null/alternative hypotheses anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two types of error distinguished

anchor.snippet: Two types of error are distinguished: type I error and type II error

annotations.note: The taxonomy of testing errors is not part of Mathlib's probability content.

annotations.label: Type I error

anchor.snippet: The first kind of error is the mistaken rejection of a null hypothesis

annotations.note: No declaration for rejection of a true null hypothesis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type II error

anchor.snippet: The second kind of error is the failure to reject the null hypothesis

annotations.note: No declaration for failure to reject a false null hypothesis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Crossover error rate (CER)

anchor.snippet: is the point at which type I errors and type II errors are equal

annotations.note: The crossover/equal-error-rate concept is an applied statistics notion absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Error equivalences

anchor.snippet: a type I error is equivalent to a false positive

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define false positive/negative terminology in a statistical sense.

annotations.label: Perfect test

anchor.snippet: A perfect test would have zero false positives and zero false negatives

annotations.note: There is no formalized notion of a statistical test or its error rates in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type I error rate

anchor.snippet: The type I error rate is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis given that it is true

annotations.note: While conditional probability exists in Mathlib, type I error rate as a testing quantity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Significance level

anchor.snippet: a prespecified bound called the significance level

annotations.note: Grep for 'significance level' returns no relevant Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Type II error rate and power

anchor.snippet: related to the power of a test , which equals 1−β

annotations.note: Statistical power of a test is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robust test statistic

anchor.snippet: A test statistic is robust if the type I error rate is controlled

annotations.note: Mathlib has no concept of test statistics or their robustness.

annotations.label: p-value interpretation

anchor.snippet: if the p-value of a test statistic result is 0.0596

annotations.note: p-values are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vehicle speed measuring

anchor.snippet: A device is set to measure the speed of passing vehicles

annotations.note: This is an applied illustrative example with no corresponding Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Statistical significance

anchor.snippet: then the result is said to be statistically significant and the null hypothesis is rejected

annotations.note: Statistical significance is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Null hypothesis

anchor.snippet: the so-called "null hypothesis" that the observed phenomena simply occur by chance

annotations.note: No formalization of the null hypothesis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type S errors

anchor.snippet: Type S errors are errors of sign

annotations.note: Type S (sign) errors are a specialized statistics concept absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type M errors

anchor.snippet: Type M errors are errors of magnitude

annotations.note: Type M (magnitude) errors are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Medical screening

anchor.snippet: Screening involves relatively cheap tests that are given to large populations

annotations.note: Medical screening is an applied domain with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Medical testing vs screening

anchor.snippet: Testing involves far more expensive, often invasive, procedures

annotations.note: Distinguishing testing from screening is an applied concept absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newborn screening

anchor.snippet: most states in the US require newborns to be screened for phenylketonuria

annotations.note: This real-world example has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Mammography false positives

anchor.snippet: the breast cancer screening procedure mammography

annotations.note: This applied medical example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Medical testing errors

anchor.snippet: False negatives and false positives are significant issues in medical testing

annotations.note: Medical testing error analysis is an applied topic absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: False positives with rare condition

anchor.snippet: False positives can also produce serious and counter-intuitive problems when the condition being searched for is rare

annotations.note: Though related to Bayes' theorem (which Mathlib has), this base-rate testing statement is not itself formalized.

annotations.label: False negatives with common condition

anchor.snippet: False negatives produce serious and counter-intuitive problems, especially when the condition being searched for is common

annotations.note: This applied base-rate statement is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Biometric matching errors

anchor.snippet: is susceptible to type I and type II errors

annotations.note: Biometric matching error examples have no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: False reject and false accept rate

anchor.snippet: The probability of type I errors is called the "false reject rate"

annotations.note: False reject/accept rates are applied biometrics concepts absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Blackstone's ratio

anchor.snippet: William Blackstone invented Blackstone's ratio of 10:1

annotations.note: This legal-philosophy example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Security screening

anchor.snippet: False positives are routinely found every day in airport security screening

annotations.note: This applied security example has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Spam filtering errors

anchor.snippet: in the case of spam filtering

annotations.note: Spam filtering error examples are not formalized in Mathlib.
